en.content:
Let $A=\{a, b, c\}$ and the relation $R$ be defined on $A$ as follows

$$R=\{(a, a),(b, c),(a, b)\}$$

Then, write minimum number of ordered pairs to be added in $R$ to make $R$ reflexive and transitive.
en.explanation:
Given relation, $R=\{(a, a),(b, c),(a, b)\}$.

To make $R$ is reflexive we must add $(b, b)$ and $(c, c)$ to $R$. Also, to make $R$ is transitive we must add $(a, c)$ to $R$.

So, minimum number of ordered pair is to be added are $(b, b),(c, c),(a, c)$.

en.content: Let $D$ be the domain of the real valued function $f$ defined by $f(x)=\sqrt{25-x^2}$. Then, write $D$.
en.explanation:
Given function is, $f(x)=\sqrt{25-x^2}$

$$\begin{aligned} & \text { For real valued of } f(x) \quad 25-x^2 \geq 0 \\ & x^2 \leq 25 \\ & -5 \leq x \leq+5 \\ \therefore\quad& D=[-5,5] \end{aligned}$$

en.content: If $f, g: R \rightarrow R$ be defined by $f(x)=2 x+1$ and $g(x)=x^2-2, \forall x \in R$, respectively. Then, find $gof$.
en.explanation:
Given that,

$$\begin{aligned} f(x)=2 x+1 \text { and } g(x) & =x^2-2, \forall x \in R \\ \therefore\quad g \circ f & =g\{f(x)\} \\ & =g(2 x+1)=(2 x+1)^2-2 \\ & =4 x^2+4 x+1-2 \\ & =4 x^2+4 x-1 \end{aligned}$$

en.content: Let $f: R \rightarrow R$ be the function defined by $f(x)=2 x-3, \forall x \in R$. Write $f^{-1}$.
en.explanation: $$\begin{aligned} \text{Given that,} \quad f(x) & =2 x-3, \forall x \in R \\ \text{Now, let }\quad y & =2 x-3 \\ 2 x & =y+3 \\ x & =\frac{y+3}{2} \\ \therefore\quad f^{-1}(x) & =\frac{x+3}{2} \end{aligned}$$

en.content: If $A=\{a, b, c, d\}$ and the function $f=\{(a, b),(b, d),(c, a),(d, c)\}$, write $f^{-1}$.
en.explanation: $$\begin{aligned} \text{Given that,}\quad A & =\{a, b, c, d\} \\ \text{and }\quad f & =\{(a, b),(b, d),(c, a),(d, c)\} \\ f^{-1} & =\{(b, a),(d, b),(a, c),(c, d)\} \end{aligned} $$

en.content: If $f: R \rightarrow R$ is defined by $f(x)=x^2-3 x+2$, write $f\{f(x)\}$.
en.explanation: $$\begin{aligned} \text{Given that,}\quad f(x) & =x^2-3 x+2 \\ \therefore\quad f\{f(x)\} & =f\left(x^2-3 x+2\right) \\ & =\left(x^2-3 x+2\right)^2-3\left(x^2-3 x+2\right)+2 \\ & =x^4+9 x^2+4-6 x^3-12 x+4 x^2-3 x^2+9 x-6+2 \\ & =x^4+10 x^2-6 x^3-3 x \\ f\{f(x)\} & =x^4-6 x^3+10 x^2-3 x \end{aligned}$$

en.content: Is $g=\{(1,1),(2,3),(3,5),(4,7)\}$ a function? If $g$ is described by $g(x)=\alpha x+\beta$, then what value should be assigned to $\alpha$ and $\beta$ ?
en.explanation:
Given that, $g=\{(1,1),(2,3),(3,5),(4,7)\}$.

Here, each element of domain has unique image. So, $g$ is a function.

Now given that,

$$\begin{aligned} g(x) & =\alpha x+\beta \\ g(1) & =\alpha+\beta \\ \alpha+\beta & =1 \quad\text{.... (i)}\\ g(2) & =2 \alpha+\beta \\ 2 \alpha+\beta & =3\quad\text{.... (ii)} \end{aligned}$$

From Eqs. (i) and (ii),

$$\begin{aligned} 2(1-\beta)+\beta & =3 \\ \Rightarrow\quad 2-2 \beta+\beta & =3 \\ \Rightarrow\quad 2-\beta & =3 \\ \beta & =-1 \\ \text{If}\quad \beta & =-1, \text { then } \alpha=2 \\ \alpha & =2, \beta=-1 \end{aligned}$$

en.content:
Are the following set of ordered pairs functions? If so examine whether the mapping is injective or surjective.

(i) $\{(x, y): x$ is a person, $y$ is the mother of $x\}$.

(ii) $\{(a, b): a$ is a person, $b$ is an ancestor of $a\}$.
en.explanation:
(i) Given set of ordered pair is $\{(x, y): x$ is a person, $y$ is the mother of $x\}$.

It represent a function. Here, the image of distinct elements of $x$ under $f$ are not distinct, so it is not a injective but it is a surjective.

(ii) Set of ordered pairs $=\{(a, b)$ : $a$ is a person, $b$ is an ancestor of $a\}$

Here, each element of domain does not have a unique image. So, it does not represent function.

en.content: If the mappings $f$ and $g$ are given by $f=\{(1,2),(3,5),(4,1)\}$ and $g=\{(2,3),(5,1),(1,3)\}$, write $fog$.
Let $C$ be the set of complex numbers. Prove that the mapping $f: C \rightarrow R$ given by $f(z)=|z|, \forall z \in C$, is neither one-one nor onto.
en.explanation:
$$\begin{aligned} \text{The mapping}\quad & f: C \rightarrow R \\ \text{Given, }\quad & f(z)=|z|, \forall z \in C \\ & f(1)=|1|=1 \\ & f(-1)=|-1|=1 \\ & f(1)=f(-1) \\ \text{But }\quad & 1 \neq-1 \end{aligned}$$

So, $f(z)$ is not one-one. Also, $f(z)$ is not onto as there is no pre-image for any negative element of $R$ under the mapping $f(z)$.

en.content: Let the function $f: R \rightarrow R$ be defined by $f(x)=\cos x, \forall x \in R$. Show that $f$ is neither one-one nor onto.
en.explanation:
Given function, $f(x)=\cos x, \forall x \in R$

Now, $$f\left(\frac{\pi}{2}\right)=\cos \frac{\pi}{2}=0$$

$$\begin{array}{lc} \Rightarrow & f\left(\frac{-\pi}{2}\right)=\cos \frac{\pi}{2}=0 \\ \Rightarrow & f\left(\frac{\pi}{2}\right)=f\left(\frac{-\pi}{2}\right) \\ \text { But } & \frac{\pi}{2} \neq \frac{-\pi}{2} \end{array}$$

So, $f(x)$ is not one-one.

Now, $f(x)=\cos x, \forall x \in R$ is not onto as there is no pre-image for any real number. Which does not belonging to the intervals $[-1,1]$, the range of $\cos x$.

Given that,

$$\begin{aligned} X & =\{1,2,3\} \text { and } Y=\{4,5\} \\ X \times Y & =\{(1,4),(1,5),(2,4),(2,5),(3,4),(3,5)\} \end{aligned}$$

(i) $f=\{(1,4),(1,5),(2,4),(3,5)\}$

$f$ is not a function because $f$ has not unique image.

(ii) $g=\{(1,4),(2,4),(3,4)\}$

Since, $g$ is a function as each element of the domain has unique image.

(iii) $h=\{(1,4),(2,5),(3,5)\}$

It is clear that $h$ is a function.

(iv) $k=\{(1,4),(2,5)\}$

$k$ is not a function as 3 has not any image under the mapping.

en.content: If functions $f: A \rightarrow B$ and $g: B \rightarrow A$ satisfy $g \circ f=I_A$, then show that $f$ is one-one and $g$ is onto.
en.explanation:
Given that,

$f: A \rightarrow B$ and $g: B \rightarrow A$ satisfy $g \circ f=I_A$

$$\begin{aligned} \because\quad g \circ f & =I_A \\ \Rightarrow\quad\operatorname{g\circ f}\left\{f\left(x_1\right)\right\} & =g \circ f\left\{f\left(x_2\right)\right\} \\ \Rightarrow\quad g\left(x_1\right) & =g\left(x_2\right) \quad \left[\because g \circ f=I_A\right]\\ \therefore\quad x_1 & =x_2 \end{aligned}$$

Hence, f is one-one and g is onto.

en.content: Let $f: R \rightarrow R$ be the function defined by $f(x)=\frac{1}{2-\cos x}, \forall x \in R$. Then, find the range of $f$.
en.explanation:
$$\begin{aligned} \text{Given function,}\quad & f(x)=\frac{1}{2-\cos x}, \forall x \in R \\ \text{Let,}\quad & y=\frac{1}{2-\cos x} \\ \Rightarrow\quad & 2 y-y \cos x=1 \\ \Rightarrow\quad & y \cos x=2 y-1 \\ \Rightarrow\quad & \cos x=\frac{2 y-1}{y}=2-\frac{1}{y} \Rightarrow \cos x=2-\frac{1}{y} \\ \Rightarrow\quad & -1 \leq \cos x \leq 1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad-1 \leq 2-\frac{1}{y} \leq 1 \\ \Rightarrow\quad & -3 \leq-\frac{1}{y} \leq-1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad 1 \leq \frac{1}{y} \leq 3 \\ \Rightarrow\quad & \frac{1}{3} \leq \frac{1}{y} \leq 1 \end{aligned}$$

So, $y$ range is $\left[\frac{1}{3}, 1\right]$.

en.content: Let $n$ be a fixed positive integer. Define a relation $R$ in $Z$ as follows $\forall a$, $b \in Z, a R b$ if and only if $a-b$ is divisible by $n$. Show that $R$ is an equivalence relation.
en.explanation:
Given that, $\forall a, b \in Z, a R b$ if and only if $a-b$ is divisible by $n$.

Now,

I. Reflexive

$a R a \Rightarrow(a-a)$ is divisible by $n$, which is true for any integer $a$ as ' $O$ ' is divisible by $n$. Hence, $R$ is reflexive.

II. Symmetric

$$\begin{array}{ll} & a R b \\ \Rightarrow & a-b \text { is divisible by } n . \\ \Rightarrow & -b+a \text { is divisible by } n \\ \Rightarrow & -(b-a) \text { is divisible by } n \\ \Rightarrow & (b-a) \text { is divisible by } n . \\ \Rightarrow & b R a \end{array}$$

Hence, R is symmetric.

III. Transitive

Let $a R b$ and $b R c$

$\Rightarrow\quad (a-b)$ is divisible by $n$ and $(b-c)$ is divisible by $n$

$\Rightarrow\quad(a-b)+(b-c)$ is divisibly by $n$

$\Rightarrow\quad(a-c)$ is divisible by $n$

$\Rightarrow\quad aRc$

Hence, R is transitive.

So, R is an equivalence relation.

en.content:
If $A=\{1,2,3,4\}$, define relations on $A$ which have properties of being

(i) reflexive, transitive but not symmetric.

(ii) symmetric but neither reflexive nor transitive.

(iii) reflexive, symmetric and transitive.
en.explanation:
Given that, $$A=\{1,2,3,4\}$$

(i) Let $$R_1=\{(1,1),(1,2),(2,3),(2,2),(1,3),(3,3)\}$$

$R_1$ is reflexive, since, $(1,1)(2,2)(3,3)$ lie in $R_1$.

Now, $$(1,2) \in R_1,(2,3) \in R_1 \Rightarrow(1,3) \in R_1$$

Hence, $R_1$ is also transitive but $(1,2) \in R_1 \Rightarrow(2,1) \notin R_1$.

So, it is not symmetric.

(ii) Let $$R_2=\{(1,2),(2,1)\}$$

Now, $$(1,2) \in R_2,(2,1) \in R_2$$

So, it is symmetric.

(iii) Let $$R_3=\{(1,2),(2,1),(1,1),(2,2),(3,3),(1,3),(3,1),(2,3)\}$$

Hence, $R_3$ is reflexive, symmetric and transitive.

en.content: Let $R$ be relation defined on the set of natural number $N$ as follows, $R=\{(x, y): x \in N, y \in N, 2 x+y=41\}$. Find the domain and range of the relation $R$. Also verify whether $R$ is reflexive, symmetric and transitive.
en.explanation:
$$\begin{aligned} \text{Given that,}\quad R & =\{(x, y): x \in N, y \in N, 2 x+y=41\} \\ \text { Domain } & =\{1,2,3, \ldots, 20\} \\ \text { Range } & =\{1,3,5,7, \ldots, 39\} \\ R & =\{(1,39),(2,37),(3,35), \ldots,(19,3),(20,1)\} \end{aligned}$$

$R$ is not reflexive as $(2,2) \notin R$

$$2 \times 2+2 \neq 41$$

So, $R$ is not symmetric.

As $$(1,39) \in R \text { but }(39,1) \notin R $$

So, $R$ is not transitive.

As $$(11,19) \in R,(19,3) \in R$$

But $(11,3) \notin R$

Hence, $R$ is neither reflexive, nor symmetric and nor transitive.

en.content:
Given, $A=\{2,3,4\}, B=\{2,5,6,7\}$. Construct an example of each of the following

(i) an injective mapping from $A$ to $B$.

(ii) a mapping from $A$ to $B$ which is not injective.

(iii) a mapping from $B$ to $A$.
en.explanation:
Given that, $$A=\{2,3,4\}, B=\{2,5,6,7\}$$

(i) Let $f: A \rightarrow B$ denote a mapping

$$\begin{aligned} & f=\{(x, y): y=x+3\} \\ \text{i.e.,}\quad & f=\{(2,5),(3,-6),(4,7)\}, \text { which is an injective mapping. } \end{aligned}$$

(ii) Let $g: A \rightarrow B$ denote a mapping such that $g=\{(2,2),(3,5),(4,5)\}$, which is not an injective mapping.

(iii) Let $h: B \rightarrow A$ denote a mapping such that $h=\{(2,2),(5,3),(6,4),(7,4)\}$, which is a mapping from $B$ to $A$.

en.content:
Give an example of a map

(i) which is one-one but not onto.

(ii) which is not one-one but onto.

(iii) which is neither one-one nor onto.
en.explanation:
(i) Let $f: N \rightarrow N$, be a mapping defined by $f(x)=2 x$ which is one-one.

$$\begin{aligned} \text{For}\quad f\left(x_1\right) & =f\left(x_2\right) \\ \Rightarrow\quad 2 x_1 & =2 x_2 \\ x_1 & =x_2 \end{aligned}$$

Further $f$ is not onto, as for $1 \in N$, there does not exist any $x$ in $N$ such that $f(x)=2 x+1$.

(ii) Let $f: N \rightarrow N$, given by $f(1)=f(2)=1$ and $f(x)=x-1$ for every $x>2$ is onto but not one-one. $f$ is not one-one as $f(1)=f(2)=1$. But $f$ is onto.

(iii) The mapping $f: R \rightarrow R$ defined as $f(x)=x^2$, is neither one-one nor onto.

en.content: Let $A=R-\{3\}, B=R-\{1\}$. If $f: A \rightarrow B$ be defined by $f(x)=\frac{x-2}{x-3}$, $\forall x \in A$. Then, show that $f$ is bijective.
en.explanation:
Given that, $$A=R-\{3\}, B=R-\{1\} .$$

$f: A \rightarrow B$ is defined by $f(x)=\frac{x-2}{x-3}, \forall x \in A$

For injectivity

Let $$f\left(x_1\right)=f\left(x_2\right) \Rightarrow \frac{x_1-2}{x_1-3}=\frac{x_2-2}{x_2-3}$$

$$\begin{aligned} &\begin{array}{rrr} \Rightarrow & \left(x_1-2\right)\left(x_2-3\right) =\left(x_2-2\right)\left(x_1-3\right) \\ \Rightarrow & x_1 x_2-3 x_1-2 x_2+6 =x_1 x_2-3 x_2-2 x_1+6 \\ \Rightarrow & -3 x_1-2 x_2 =-3 x_2-2 x_1 \\ \Rightarrow & -x_1 =-x_2 \Rightarrow x_1=x_2 \end{array}\\ &\text { So, } f(x) \text { is an injective function. } \end{aligned}$$

For surjectivity

$$\begin{aligned} \text{Let}\quad y & =\frac{x-2}{x-3} \Rightarrow x-2=x y-3 y \\ \Rightarrow\quad x(1-y) & =2-3 y \Rightarrow x=\frac{2-3 y}{1-y} \\ \Rightarrow\quad x & =\frac{3 y-2}{y-1} \in A, \forall y \in B\quad\text{[codomain]} \end{aligned}$$

So, $f(x)$ is surjective function.

Hence, $f(x)$ is a bijective function.

en.content:
Let $A=[-1,1]$, then, discuss whether the following functions defined on $A$ are one-one onto or bijective.

(i) $f(x)=\frac{x}{2}\quad$ (ii) $g(x)=|x|$

(iii) $h(x)=x|x|\quad$ (iv) $k(x)=x^2$
en.explanation:
Given that, $$A=[-1,1]$$

(i) $f(x)=\frac{x}{2}$

$$\begin{array}{ll} \text { Let } & f\left(x_1\right)=f\left(x_2\right) \\ \Rightarrow & \frac{x_1}{2}=\frac{x_2}{2} \Rightarrow x_1=x_2 \end{array}$$

So, $f(x)$ is one-one.

Now, let $$y=\frac{x}{2}$$

$$\begin{array}{ll} \Rightarrow & x=2 y \notin A, \forall y \in A \\ \text { As for } & y=1 \in A, x=2 \notin A \end{array}$$

So, $f(x)$ is not onto.

Also, $f(x)$ is not bijective as it is not onto.

(ii) $g(x)=|x|$

Let $$g\left(x_1\right)=g\left(x_2\right)$$

So, $g(x)$ is not one-one.

Now, $\quad y=|x| \Rightarrow x= \pm y \notin A, \forall y \in A$

So, $g(x)$ is not onto, also, $g(x)$ is not bijective.

(iii) $h(x)=x|x|$

$$\begin{aligned} &\begin{aligned} \text { Let } \quad h\left(x_1\right) & =h\left(x_2\right) \\ \Rightarrow\quad x_1\left|x_1\right| & =x_2\left|x_2\right| \quad \Rightarrow x_1=x_2 \end{aligned} \end{aligned}$$

So, $h(x)$ is one-one.

$$\begin{array}{ll} \text { Now, let } & y=x|x| \\ \Rightarrow & y=x^2 \in A, \forall x \in A \end{array}$$

So, $h(x)$ is onto also, $h(x)$ is a bijective.

(iv) $k(x)=x^2$

$$\begin{aligned} &\begin{aligned} \text { Let }\quad k\left(x_1\right) & =k\left(x_2\right) \\ x_1^2 & =x_2^2 \Rightarrow x_1= \pm x_2 \end{aligned} \end{aligned}$$

Thus, $k(x)$ is not one-one.

Now, let $$y=x^2$$

$$\Rightarrow \quad x=\sqrt{y} \notin A, \forall y \in A$$

As for $y=-1, x=\sqrt{-1} \notin A$

Hence, $k(x)$ is neither one-one nor onto.

en.content:
Each of the following defines a relation of $N$

(i) $x$ is greater than $y, x, y \in N$.

(ii) $x+y=10, x, y \in N$.

(iii) $x y$ is square of an integer $x, y \in N$.

(iv) $x+4 y=10, x, y \in N$

Determine which of the above relations are reflexive, symmetric and transitive.
en.explanation:
$$\begin{array}{r} \text { (i) } x \text { is greater than } y, x, y \in N \\ (x, x) \in R \end{array}$$

For $x R x \quad x>x$ is not true for any $x \in N$.

Therefore, $R$ is not reflexive.

$$\begin{aligned} \text{Let}\quad (x, y) & \in R \Rightarrow x R y \\ x & >y \end{aligned}$$

but $y>x$ is not true for any $x, y \in N$

Thus, $R$ is not symmetric.

Let $x R y$ and $y R z$

$x>y$ and $y>z \Rightarrow x>z$

$x R z$

So, R is transitive.

(ii) $x+y=10, x, y \in N$

$$\begin{aligned} & R=\{(x, y) ; x+y=10, x, y \in \mathcal{N}\} \\ & R=\{(1,9),(2,8),(3,7),(4,6),(5,5),(6,4),(7,3),(8,2),(9,1)\}(1,1) \notin R \end{aligned}$$

So, $R$ is not reflexive.

$$(x, y) \in R \quad \Rightarrow \quad(y, x) \in R$$

Therefore, $R$ is symmetric.

$$(1,9) \in R,(9,1) \in R \quad \Rightarrow \quad(1,1) \notin R$$

Hence, $R$ is not transitive.

(iii) Given $x y$, is square of an integer $x, y \in N$.

$$\Rightarrow \quad \begin{aligned} R & =\{(x, y): x y \text { is a square of an integer } x, y \in N\} \\ (x, x) & \in R, \forall x \in N \end{aligned}$$

As $x^2$ is square of an integer for any $x \in N$.

Hence, $R$ is reflexive.

If $$(x, y) \in R \Rightarrow(y, x) \in R$$

Therefore, $R$ is symmetric.

If $$(x, y) \in R,(y, z) \in R$$

So, $x y$ is square of an integer and $y z$ is square of an integer.

Let $x y=m^2$ and $y z=n^2$ for some $m, n \in Z$

$$\begin{aligned} & x=\frac{m^2}{y} \text { and } z=\frac{x^2}{y} \\ & x z=\frac{m^2 n^2}{y^2}, \text { which is square of an integer. } \end{aligned}$$

So, $R$ is transitive.

$$\begin{aligned} &\begin{aligned} \text { (iv) } & x+4 y=10, x, y \in N \\ & R=\{(x, y): x+4 y=10, x, y \in N\} \\ & R=\{(2,2),(6,1)\} \\ &(1,1),(3,3), \ldots, \notin R \end{aligned} \end{aligned}$$

Thus, $R$ is not reflexive.

$$(6,1) \in R \text { but }(1,6) \notin R$$

Hence, $R$ is not symmetric.

$$\begin{aligned} (x, y) \in R & \Rightarrow x+4 y=10 \text { but }(y, z) \in R \\ y+4 z=10 & \Rightarrow(x, z) \in R \end{aligned}$$

So, $R$ is transitive.

en.content: Let $A=\{1,2,3, \ldots, 9\}$ and $R$ be the relation in $A \times A$ defined by $(a, b) R(c, d)$ if $a+d=b+c$ for $(a, b),(c, d)$ in $A \times A$. Prove that $R$ is an equivalence relation and also obtain the equivalent class $[(2,5)]$.
en.explanation:
Given that, $A=\{1,2,3, \ldots, 9\}$ and $(a, b) R(c, d)$ if $a+d=b+c$ for $(a, b) \in A \times A$ and $(c, d) \in A \times A$.

Let $(a, b) R(a, b)$

$$\Rightarrow \quad a+b=b+a, \forall a, b \in A$$

which is true for any $a, b \in A$.

Hence, $R$ is reflexive.

Let $(a, b) R(c, d)$

$$\begin{aligned} & a+d=b+c \\ & c+b=d+a \quad \Rightarrow \quad(c, d) R(a, b) \end{aligned}$$

So, $R$ is symmetric.

$$\begin{aligned} &\text { Let }\\ &\begin{aligned} (a, b) R(c, d) \text { and } & (c, d) R(e, f) \\ a+d & =b+c \text { and } c+f=d+e \\ a+d & =b+c \text { and } d+e=c+f \\ (a+d)-(d+e) & =(b+c)-(c+f) \\ (a-e) & =b-f \\ a+f & =b+e \\ (a, b) & R(e, f) \end{aligned} \end{aligned}$$

So, $R$ is transitive.

Hence, $R$ is an equivalence relation.

Now, equivalence class containing $[(2,5)]$ is $\{(1,4),(2,5),(3,6),(4,7),(5,8),(6,9)\}$.

en.content: Using the definition, prove that the function $f: A \rightarrow B$ is invertible if and only if $f$ is both one-one and onto.
en.explanation: A function $f: X \rightarrow Y$ is defined to be invertible, if there exist a function $g=Y \rightarrow X$ such that gof $=I_X$ and fog $=I_Y$. The function is called the inverse of $f$ and is denoted by $f^{-1}$. A function $f=X \rightarrow Y$ is invertible iff $f$ is a bijective function.

en.content:
Functions $f, g: R \rightarrow R$ are defined, respectively, by $f(x)=x^2+3 x+1$, $g(x)=2 x-3$, find

(i) $f \circ g$ (ii) $g \circ f$ (iii) $f \circ f$ (iv) $g \circ g$
en.explanation:
Given that,

$$f(x)=x^2+3 x+1, g(x)=2 x-3$$

(i)

$$\begin{aligned} f \circ g & =f\{g(x)\}=f(2 x-3) \\ & =(2 x-3)^2+3(2 x-3)+1 \\ & =4 x^2+9-12 x+6 x-9+1=4 x^2-6 x+1 \end{aligned}$$

(ii)

$$\begin{aligned} g \circ f & =g\{f(x)\}=g\left(x^2+3 x+1\right) \\ & =2\left(x^2+3 x+1\right)-3 \\ & =2 x^2+6 x+2-3=2 x^2+6 x-1 \end{aligned}$$

(iii)

$$\begin{aligned} f \circ f & =f\{f(x)\}=f\left(x^2+3 x+1\right) \\ & =\left(x^2+3 x+1\right)^2+3\left(x^2+3 x+1\right)+1 \\ & =x^4+9 x^2+1+6 x^3+6 x+2 x^2+3 x^2+9 x+3+1 \\ & =x^4+6 x^3+14 x^2+15 x+5 \end{aligned}$$

(iv)

$$\begin{aligned} g \circ g & =g\{g(x)\}=g(2 x-3) \\ & =2(2 x-3)-3 \\ & =4 x-6-3=4 x-9 \end{aligned}$$

en.content:
Let * be the binary operation defined on $Q$. Find which of the following binary operations are commutative

(i) $a * b=a-b, \forall a, b \in Q$

(ii) $a * b=a^2+b^2, \forall a, b \in Q$

(iii) $a * b=a+a b, \forall a, b \in Q$

(iv) $a * b=(a-b)^2, \forall a, b \in Q$
en.explanation:
Given that * be the binary operation defined on $Q$.

(i) $a * b=a-b, \forall a, b \in Q$ and $b * a=b-a$

So, $$a * b \neq b * a\quad$$ $$[\because b-a \neq a-b]$$

Hence, * is not commutative.

(ii)

$$\begin{aligned} & a * b=a^2+b^2 \\ & b * a=b^2+a^2 \end{aligned}$$

So, * is commutative.

[since, ' + ' is on rational is commutative]

$$\begin{aligned} &\text { (iii) }\\ &\begin{aligned} a * b & =a+a b \\ b * a & =b+a b \\ \text { Clearly, } \quad a+a b & \neq b+a b \end{aligned}\\ &\text { So, * is not commutative. } \end{aligned}$$

(iv)

$$\begin{aligned} a * b & =(a-b)^2, \forall a, b \in Q \\ b * a & =(b-a)^2 \\ \because \quad(a-b)^2 & =(b-a)^2 \end{aligned}$$

Hence, * is commutative.

en.content:
If * be binary operation defined on $R$ by $a * b=1+a b, \forall a, b \in R$. Then, the operation $*$ is

(i) commutative but not associative.

(ii) associative but not commutative.

(iii) neither commutative nor associative.

(iv) both commutative and associative.
en.explanation:
$$\begin{aligned} &\text { (i) Given that, }\\ &\begin{aligned} & a * b=1+a b, \forall a, b \in R \\ & a * b=a b+1=b * a \end{aligned} \end{aligned}$$

So, * is a commutative binary operation.

Also,

$$\begin{aligned} a *(b * c) & =a *(1+b c)=1+a(1+b c) \\ a *(b * c) & =1+a+a b c \quad\text{.... (i)}\\ (a * b) * c & =(1+a b) * c \\ & =1+(1+a b) c=1+c+a b c\quad\text{.... (ii)} \end{aligned}$$

So, * is not associative.

Hence, * is commutative but not associative.

en.content: Let $T$ be the set of all triangles in the Euclidean plane and let a relation $R$ on $T$ be defined as $a R b$, if $a$ is congruent to $b, \forall a, b \in T$. Then, $R$ is
en.explanation:
Consider that $a R b$, if $a$ is congruent to $b, \forall a, b \in T$.

Then, $\quad a R a \Rightarrow a \cong a$, which is true for all $a \in T$

So, $R$ is reflexive,$\quad\text{.... (i)}$

$$\begin{aligned} &\text { Let }\\ &\begin{array}{r} a R b \Rightarrow a \cong b \\ \Rightarrow\quad b \cong a \Rightarrow b \cong a \end{array}\\ &\Rightarrow \quad b R a\quad\text{.... (ii)} \end{aligned}$$

So, R is symmetric.

Let aRb and bRc

$$\begin{array}{ll} \Rightarrow & a \cong b \text { and } b \cong c \\ \Rightarrow & a \cong c \Rightarrow a R c \end{array}$$

So, R is transitive. $\quad\text{.... (iii)}$

Hence, R is equivalence relation.

en.content: Consider the non-empty set consisting of children in a family and a relation $R$ defined as $a R b$, if $a$ is brother of $b$. Then, $R$ is
en.explanation:
$$\begin{array}{ll} \text { Given, } & a R b \Rightarrow a \text { is brother of } b \\ \therefore & a R a \Rightarrow a \text { is brother of } a \text {, which is not true. } \end{array}$$

$$\begin{aligned} &\text { So, } R \text { is not reflexive. }\\ &a R b \Rightarrow a \text { is brother of } b \text {. } \end{aligned}$$

This does not mean $b$ is also a brother of $a$ and $b$ can be a sister of $a$. Hence, $R$ is not symmetric.

$a R b \Rightarrow a$ is brother of $b$

and $\quad b R c \Rightarrow b$ is a brother of $c$.

So, a is brother of c.

Hence, R is transitive.

en.content: The maximum number of equivalence relations on the set $A=\{1,2,3\}$ are
en.explanation:
Given that, $A=\{1,2,3\}$

Now, number of equivalence relations as follows

$$\begin{aligned} & R_1=\{(1,1),(2,2),(3,3)\} \\ & R_2=\{(1,1),(2,2),(3,3),(1,2),(2,1)\} \\ & R_3=\{(1,1),(2,2),(3,3),(1,3),(3,1)\} \\ & R_4=\{(1,1),(2,2),(3,3),(2,3),(3,2)\} \\ & R_5=\left\{(1,2,3) \Leftrightarrow A \times A=A^2\right\} \end{aligned}$$

$\therefore$ Maximum number of equivalence relation on the set $A=\{1,2,3\}=5$

en.content: If a relation $R$ on the set $\{1,2,3\}$ be defined by $R=\{(1,2)\}$, then $R$ is
en.explanation:
$R$ on the set $\{1,2,3\}$ be defined by $R=\{(1,2)\}$

It is clear that $R$ is transitive.

en.content: Let us define a relation $R$ in $R$ as $a R b$ if $a \geq b$. Then, $R$ is

options.content: neither transitive nor reflexive but symmetric

en.explanation:
Given that,

$a R b$ if $a \geq b$

$\Rightarrow \quad a R a \Rightarrow a \geq a$ which is true.

Let $a R b, a \geq b$, then $b \geq a$ which is not true $R$ is not symmetric.

But $a R b$ and $b R c$

$$\begin{array}{ll} \Rightarrow & a \geq b \text { and } b \geq c \\ \Rightarrow & a \geq c \end{array}$$

Hence, $R$ is transitive.

Given that,

$$A=\{1,2,3\}$$

and $R=\{(1,1),(2,2),(3,3),(1,2),(2,3),(1,3)\}$

$$\because \quad(1,1),(2,2),(3,3) \in R$$

Hence, $R$ is reflexive.

$$(1,2) \in R \text { but }(2,1) \notin R$$

Hence, $R$ is not symmetric.

$$\begin{array}{ll} & (1,2) \in R \text { and }(2,3) \in R \\ & (1,3) \in R \end{array}$$

Hence, $R$ is transitive.

en.content: The identity element for the binary operation $*$ defined on $Q-\{0\}$ as $a * b=\frac{a b}{2}, \forall a, b \in Q-\{0\}$ is
en.explanation:
Given that, $a * b=\frac{a b}{2}, \forall a, b \in Q-\{0\}$.

Let $e$ be the identity element for *.

$$\begin{array}{ll} \therefore & a * e=\frac{a e}{2} \\ \Rightarrow & a=\frac{a e}{2} \Rightarrow e=2 \end{array}$$

en.content: If the set $A$ contains 5 elements and the set $B$ contains 6 elements, then the number of one-one and onto mappings from $A$ to $B$ is
en.explanation:
We know that, if $A$ and $B$ are two non-empty finite set containing $m$ and $n$ elements respectively, then the number of one-one and onto mapping from $A$ to $B$ is

$$\begin{aligned} n!\text { if } m & =n \\ 0, \text { if } m & \neq n \\ \text{Given that, }\quad m & =5 \text { and } n=6 \\ \therefore\quad m & \neq n \end{aligned}$$

Number of mapping $=0$

en.content: If $A=\{1,2,3, \ldots, n\}$ and $B=\{a, b\}$. Then, the number of surjections from $A$ into $B$ is
en.explanation:
Given that, $A=\{1,2,3, \ldots, n\}$ and $B=\{a, b\}$.

We know that, if $A$ and $B$ are two non-empty finite sets containing $m$ and $n$ elements respectively, then the number of surjection from $A$ into $B$ is

$$\begin{array}{r} { }^n C_m \times m!\text {, if } n \geq m \\ 0, \text { if } n< m \end{array}$$

Here, $m=2$

$\therefore \quad$ Number of surjection from $A$ into $B$ is ${ }^n C_2 \times 2!=\frac{n!}{2!(n-2)!} \times 2!$

$$=\frac{n(n-1)(n-2)!}{2 \times 1(n-2)} \times 2!=n^2-n$$

en.content: If $f: R \rightarrow R$ be defined by $f(x)=\frac{1}{x}, \forall x \in R$. Then, $f$ is
en.explanation:
$$\begin{aligned} \text{Given that,}\quad f(x) & =\frac{1}{x}, \forall x \in R \\ \text{For}\quad x & =0 \end{aligned}$$

$f(x)$ is not defined.

Hence, $f(x)$ is a not define function.

en.content: If $f: R \rightarrow R$ be defined by $f(x)=3 x^2-5$ and $g: R \rightarrow R$ by $g(x)=\frac{x}{x^2+1}$. Then, $g \circ f$ is
en.explanation: $$\begin{aligned} &\text { Given that, }\\ &\begin{aligned} f(x) & =3 x^2-5 \text { and } g(x)=\frac{x}{x^2+1} \\ g \circ f & =g\{f(x)\}=g\left(3 x^2-5\right) \\ & =\frac{3 x^2-5}{\left(3 x^2-5\right)^2+1}=\frac{3 x^2-5}{9 x^4-30 x^2+25+1} \\ & =\frac{3 x^2-5}{9 x^4-30 x^2+26} \end{aligned} \end{aligned}$$

en.content: Which of the following functions from $Z$ into $Z$ are bijections?
en.explanation:
$$\text{Here,}\quad f(x)=x+2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad f\left(x_1\right)=f\left(x_2\right)$$

$$x_1+2=x_2+2 \Rightarrow x_1=x_2$$

$$\begin{aligned} \text{Let }\quad & y=x+2 \\ & x=y-2 \in Z, \forall y \in x \end{aligned}$$

Hence, $f(x)$ is one-one and onto.

en.content: If $f: R \rightarrow R$ be the functions defined by $f(x)=x^3+5$, then $f^{-1}(x)$ is
en.explanation: $$\begin{aligned} \text{Given that,}\quad f(x) & =x^3+5 \\ \text{Let}\quad y & =x^3+5 \quad \Rightarrow x^3=y-5 \\ x & =(y-5)^{\frac{1}{3}} \Rightarrow f(x)^{-1}=(x-5)^{\frac{1}{3}} \end{aligned}$$

en.content: If $f: A \rightarrow B$ and $g: B \rightarrow C$ be the bijective functions, then $(g \circ f)^{-1}$ is
en.explanation:
Given that, $f: A \rightarrow B$ and $g: B \rightarrow C$ be the bijective functions.

$$(g \circ f)^{-1}=f^{-1} \circ g^{-1}$$

en.content: If $f: R-\left\{\frac{3}{5}\right\} \rightarrow R$ be defined by $f(x)=\frac{3 x+2}{5 x-3}$, then
en.explanation: $$\begin{aligned} \text{Given that,}\quad f(x) & =\frac{3 x+2}{5 x-3} \\ \text{Let}\quad y & =\frac{3 x+2}{5 x-3} \\ 3 x+2 & =5 x y-3 y \Rightarrow x(3-5 y)=-3 y-2 \\ x & =\frac{3 y+2}{5 y-3} \Rightarrow f^{-1}(x)=\frac{3 x+2}{5 x-3} \\ \therefore\quad f^{-1}(x) & =f(x) \end{aligned}$$

en.content: If $f:[0,1] \rightarrow[0,1]$ be defined by $f(x)=\left\{\begin{array}{cc}x, & \text { if } x \text { is rational } \\ 1-x, & \text { if } x \text { is irrational }\end{array}\right.$ then $(f \circ f) x$ is
en.explanation: $$\begin{aligned} &\text { Given that, } f:[0,1] \rightarrow[0,1] \text { be defined by }\\ &\begin{array}{ll} & f(x)=\left\{\begin{array}{cc} x, & \text { if } x \text { is rational } \\ 1-x, & \text { if } x \text { is irrational } \end{array}\right. \\ \therefore & (f \circ f) x=f(f(x))=x \end{array} \end{aligned}$$

en.content: If $f:[2, \infty) \rightarrow R$ be the function defined by $f(x)=x^2-4 x+5$, then the range of $f$ is
en.explanation:
Given that, $$ f(x)=x^2-4 x+5 $$

$$\begin{array}{lrl} \text { Let } & y & =x^2-4 x+5 \\ \Rightarrow & y & =x^2-4 x+4+1=(x-2)^2+1 \\ \Rightarrow & (x-2)^2 & =y-1 \Rightarrow x-2=\sqrt{y-1} \\ \Rightarrow & x & =2+\sqrt{y-1} \\ \therefore & y-1 & \geq 0, y \geq 1 \end{array}$$

$$\text { Range }=[1, \infty)$$

en.content: If $f: N \rightarrow R$ be the function defined by $f(x)=\frac{2 x-1}{2}$ and $g: Q \rightarrow R$ be another function defined by $g(x)=x+2$. Then, $(g \circ f) \frac{3}{2}$ is
en.explanation:
Given that, $f(x)=\frac{2 x-1}{2}$ and $g(x)=x+2$

$$\begin{aligned} (g \circ f) \frac{3}{2} & =g\left[f\left(\frac{3}{2}\right)\right]=g\left(\frac{2 \times \frac{3}{2}-1}{2}\right) \\ & =g(1)=1+2=3 \end{aligned}$$

en.content: If $f: R \rightarrow R$ be defined by $f(x)=\left\{\begin{array}{l}2 x: x>3 \\ x^2: 1< x \leq 3 \\ 3 x: x \leq 1\end{array}\right.$ Then, $f(-1)+f(2)+f(4)$ is
en.explanation:
Given that, $f(x)=\left\{\begin{array}{l}2 x: x > 3 \\ x^2: 1< x \leq 3 \\ 3 x: x \leq 1\end{array}\right.$

$$\begin{aligned} f(-1)+f(2)+f(4) & =3(-1)+(2)^2+2 \times 4 \\ & =-3+4+8=9 \end{aligned}$$

en.content: If $f: R \rightarrow R$ be given by $f(x)=\tan x$, then $f^{-1}(1)$ is
en.explanation:
$$\begin{aligned} \text{Given that,}\quad f(x) & =\tan x \\ \text{Let }\quad y & =\tan x \quad \Rightarrow \quad x=\tan ^{-1} y \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{array}{rlr} \Rightarrow & f^{-1}(x) =\tan ^{-1} x \Rightarrow f^{-1}(1)=\tan ^{-1} 1 \\ \Rightarrow & =\tan ^{-1} \tan \frac{\pi}{4}=\frac{\pi}{4} \quad {\left[\because \tan \frac{\pi}{4}=1\right]} \end{array}$$

en.content: Let the relation $R$ be defined in $N$ by $a R b$, if $2 a+3 b=30$. Then, $R=$ ..........
en.explanation:
$$\begin{aligned} \text{Given that,}\quad 2 a+3 b & =30 \\ 3 b & =30-2 a \\ b & =\frac{30-2 a}{3} \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned} \text{For}\quad & a=3, b=8 \\ & a=6, b=6 \\ & a=9, b=4 \\ & a=12, b=2 \\ & R=\{(3,8),(6,6),(9,4),(12,2)\} \end{aligned}$$

en.content: If the relation $R$ be defined on the set $A=\{1,2,3,4,5\}$ by $R=\left\{(a, b):\left|a^2-b^2\right|< 8\right\}$. Then, $R$ is given by ............ .
If $f=\{(1,2),(3,5),(4,1)\}$ and $g=\{(2,3),(5,1),(1,3)\}$, then $g \circ f=\ldots \ldots \ldots$ and $f \circ g=\ldots \ldots \ldots$.
If $f: R \rightarrow R$ be defined by $f(x)=\frac{x}{\sqrt{1+x^2}}$, then $(f \circ f \circ f)(x)=$ .............. .
en.explanation: $$\begin{aligned} \text{Given that,}\quad f(x) & =\frac{x}{\sqrt{1+x^2}} \\ (f \circ f \circ f)(x) & =f[f\{f(x)\}] \\ & =f\left[f\left(\frac{x}{\sqrt{1+x^2}}\right)\right]=f\left(\frac{\sqrt{1+x^2}}{\sqrt{1+\frac{x^2}{1+x^2}}}\right) \\ & =f\left[\frac{x \sqrt{1+x^2}}{\sqrt{1+x^2\left(\sqrt{2 x^2}+1\right)}}\right]=f\left(\frac{x}{\sqrt{1+2 x^2}}\right) \\ & =\frac{\sqrt{\sqrt{1+2 x^2}}}{\sqrt{1+\frac{x^2}{1+2 x^2}}}=\frac{x \sqrt{1+2 x^2}}{\sqrt{1+2 x^2} \sqrt{1+3 x^2}} \\ & =\frac{x}{\sqrt{1+3 x^2}}=\frac{x}{\sqrt{3 x^2+1}} \end{aligned}$$

Given that,

$$\begin{aligned} f(x) & =\left\{4-(x-7)^3\right\} \\ \text{Let}\quad y & =\left[4-(x-7)^3\right] \end{aligned}$$

$(x-7)^3=4-y$

$(x-7)=(4-y)^{1 / 3}$

$\Rightarrow\quad x=7+(4-y)^{1 / 3}$

$f^{-1}(x)=7+(4-x)^{1 / 3}$

en.content: Let $R=\{(3,1),(1,3),(3,3)\}$ be a relation defined on the set $A=\{1,2,3\}$. Then, $R$ is symmetric, transitive but not reflexive.
en.explanation:
Given that, $R=\{(3,1),(1,3),(3,3)\}$ be defined on the set $A=\{1,2,3\}$

So, $R$ is not reflexive.

$$\begin{aligned} & (1,1) \notin R \\ & (3,1) \in R,(1,3) \in R \end{aligned}$$

Hence, $R$ is symmetric.

Since, $$(3,1) \in R,(1,3) \in R$$

But $(1,1) \notin R$

Hence, $R$ is not transitive.

en.content: If $f: R \rightarrow R$ be the function defined by $f(x)=\sin (3 x+2) \forall x \in R$. Then, $f$ is invertible.
en.explanation:
Given that, $f(x)=\sin (3 x+2), \forall x \in R$ is not one-one function for all $x \in R$.

So, $f$ is not invertible.

en.content: Every relation which is symmetric and transitive is also reflexive.
en.explanation:
Let $R$ be a relation defined by $R=\{(1,2),(2,1),(1,1),(2,2)\}$ on the set $A=\{1,2,3\}$

It is clear that $(3,3) \notin R$. So, it is not reflexive.

en.content: An integer $m$ is said to be related to another integer $n$, if $m$ is a integral multiple of $n$. This relation in $Z$ is reflexive, symmetric and transitive.
en.explanation: The given relation is reflexive and transitive but not symmetric.

en.content: If $A=\{0,1\}$ and $N$ be the set of natural numbers. Then, the mapping $f: N \rightarrow A$ defined by $f(2 n-1)=0, f(2 n)=1, \forall n \in N$, is onto.
en.explanation:
Given, $$ \begin{aligned} A & =\{0,1\} \\ f(2 n-1) & =0, f(2 n)=1, \forall n \in N \end{aligned}$$

So, the mapping $f: N \rightarrow A$ is onto.

en.content: The relation $R$ on the set $A=\{1,2,3\}$ defined as $R=\{(1,1),(1,2),(2$, $1),(3,3)\}$ is reflexive, symmetric and transitive.
en.explanation:
Given that,

$$\begin{aligned} R & =\{(1,1),(1,2),(2,1),(3,3)\} \\ (2,2) & \notin R \end{aligned}$$

So, $R$ is not reflexive.

en.content: The composition of function is commutative.
en.explanation: $$\begin{aligned} \text{Let}\quad f(x) & =x^2 \\ \text{and}\quad g(x) & =x+1 \\ f \circ g(x) & =f\{g(x)\}=f(x+1) \\ & =(x+1)^2=x^2+2 x+1 \\ g \circ f(x) & =g\{f(x)\}=g\left(x^2\right)=x^2+1 \\ \therefore\quad f \circ g(x) & \neq g \circ f(x) \end{aligned} $$

en.content: The composition of function is associative.
en.explanation:
$$\begin{aligned} \text{Let}\quad& f(x)=x, g(x)=x+1 \\ \text{and}\quad& h(x)=2 x-1 \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned} \text{Then,}\quad f \circ\{g \circ h(x)\} & =f[g\{h(x)\}] \\ & =f\{g(2 x-1)\} \\ & =f(2 x-1+1) \\ & =f(2 x)=2 x \end{aligned} $$ $$ \begin{aligned} \therefore \quad(f \circ g) \circ h(x) & =(f \circ g)\{h(x)\} \\ & =(f \circ g)(2 x-1) \\ & =f\{g(2 x-1)\} \\ & =f(2 x-1+1) \\ & =f(2 x)=2 x \end{aligned}$$

en.content: Every function is invertible.
en.explanation: Only bijective functions are invertible.

en.content: A binary operation on a set has always the identity element.
en.explanation: '+' is a binary operation on the set N but it has no identity element.
